---
title: "13 · Value Iteration"
subtitle: "Iterate the BOE operator T* to convergence — the simplest planning algorithm."
lesson-id: "mfrl.value-iteration"
tier: "mfrl"
prerequisites: ["mfrl.boe-uniqueness"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "systems", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 30
status: "done"
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

Before you start:

1. Can you state the BOE in one line, and the fact (Lesson 12) that the BOE
   operator $T^*$ is a $\gamma$-contraction?
2. Can you compute one BOE update by hand on a 2- or 3-state MDP?
3. Are you comfortable extracting a [greedy](/glossary.qmd#greedy) policy
   from a value function $v$ via $\pi(s) = \arg\max_a [r + \gamma \sum P\,v]$?

If any of these is shaky, revisit Lessons 9-12.
:::

## Touch

Below is a 3×3 grid world. Goal cell at $(2,2)$ gives reward $+1$ and is
absorbing; every other step costs $0$. Discount $\gamma = 0.9$. Actions:
up/down/left/right (deterministic; bumping into a wall keeps you put).

Slide to advance the iteration. The heatmap shows $v_k(s)$ at each cell, and
the arrows show the greedy policy implied by the current $v_k$.

```{ojs}
viewof iter = Inputs.range([0, 30], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "iteration k"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  const N = 3;
  const goal = [2, 2];
  const gamma = 0.9;
  // States are (i, j) with i = row, j = col.
  // Actions: 0=up, 1=down, 2=left, 3=right
  const next = (i, j, a) => {
    if (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) return [i, j];
    let ni = i, nj = j;
    if (a === 0 && i > 0) ni = i - 1;
    if (a === 1 && i < N - 1) ni = i + 1;
    if (a === 2 && j > 0) nj = j - 1;
    if (a === 3 && j < N - 1) nj = j + 1;
    return [ni, nj];
  };
  const reward = (i, j) => (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1] ? 1 : 0);

  // Iterate
  let v = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
  for (let k = 0; k < iter; k++) {
    const vnew = Array.from({length: N}, () => Array(N).fill(0));
    for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
      let best = -Infinity;
      for (let a = 0; a < 4; a++) {
        const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
        const q = reward(ni, nj) + gamma * v[ni][nj];
        if (q > best) best = q;
      }
      vnew[i][j] = best;
    }
    v = vnew;
  }

  const greedy = (i, j) => {
    let best = -Infinity, ba = 0;
    for (let a = 0; a < 4; a++) {
      const [ni, nj] = next(i, j, a);
      const q = reward(ni, nj) + gamma * v[ni][nj];
      if (q > best) { best = q; ba = a; }
    }
    return ["↑", "↓", "←", "→"][ba];
  };

  const cells = [];
  let vmax = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) vmax = Math.max(vmax, v[i][j]);
  for (let i = 0; i < N; i++) {
    for (let j = 0; j < N; j++) {
      const intensity = vmax > 0 ? Math.round((v[i][j] / vmax) * 200) : 0;
      const colour = `rgb(${255 - intensity}, ${255 - intensity / 2}, 255)`;
      const arrow = (i === goal[0] && j === goal[1]) ? "★" : greedy(i, j);
      cells.push(html`<div style="background:${colour}; width:80px; height:80px; display:flex; flex-direction:column; align-items:center; justify-content:center; font-family:monospace; border:1px solid #999;">
        <div style="font-size: 0.85rem;">${v[i][j].toFixed(3)}</div>
        <div style="font-size: 1.4rem;">${arrow}</div>
      </div>`);
    }
  }
  return html`<div style="display:grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(${N}, 80px); gap:0; width:max-content; margin:1rem auto;">${cells}</div>
  <div style="text-align:center; font-size:0.9rem; color:#666;">k = ${iter}: cell shows v(s); arrow shows greedy action.</div>`;
}
```

Move the slider from $k = 0$ to $k = 30$. Watch the values bloom outward
from the goal, and the arrows snap into a coherent policy. Notice that the
arrows *stop changing* long before the values stop changing.

## Wonder

1. The arrows lock in early — say by $k = 5$ — but the numbers keep
   ticking up. Why does the policy stabilise before the values?
2. If you doubled $\gamma$ from 0.9 to (almost) 1.0, would the heatmap look
   different at convergence? Would it take more or fewer steps?
3. The iteration uses *yesterday's* $v$ to compute *today's* $v$. Could you
   instead use today's partial values immediately as you sweep — and would
   it still converge?

## Discover

### Problem 1 — One step of the BOE operator on the grid

For the grid world above, take $v_0 \equiv 0$ (zero everywhere). Compute
$v_1 = T^* v_0$. Which cells have a non-zero value? What is it?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$T^* v_0(s) = \max_a [r(s', a) + \gamma \cdot 0]$ where $s' = $ next state.
The reward is 1 only at the goal. So $v_1(s) > 0$ only when *some* action
from $s$ lands on the goal.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
The neighbours of the goal $(2, 2)$ are $(1, 2)$ (above) and $(2, 1)$
(left). Each has at least one action — "down" from $(1,2)$, "right" from
$(2,1)$ — that lands on the goal. So $v_1(1, 2) = v_1(2, 1) = 1$. The goal
itself is absorbing with reward 1 each step from the goal, so $v_1(2, 2) = 1$
as well. All other cells have $v_1 = 0$. **The first step lights up only
states adjacent to a reward.**
</details>

### Problem 2 — Two steps

Compute $v_2 = T^* v_1$. Which new cells become non-zero? What's
$v_2(0, 2)$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
A cell at distance 2 from the goal can reach a cell that has $v_1 = 1$ in
one step, picking up $\gamma \cdot 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$(0, 2)$ can move down to $(1, 2)$ which has $v_1 = 1$, giving
$v_2(0, 2) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 1 = 0.9$. Similarly $(1, 1) \to (1, 2)$ or
$(2, 1)$, $v_2(1, 1) = 0.9$. Cells $(1, 2)$ and $(2, 1)$ now also see *each
other's* updated value: $v_2(1, 2) = \max(\ldots, 0 + 0.9 \cdot 1) = $ bigger
than just 1 in some cases — actually still $1$ here (the absorbing goal step
dominates). **Information from the goal propagates outward by one cell per
iteration.**
</details>

### Problem 3 — When does the policy stabilise?

Use the widget. Note the iteration $k^*$ at which the *arrows* stop
changing (compared with $k^* + 1$). Compare with $k^{**}$ — when the values
stop changing (to the third decimal). Which is bigger? By how much?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Read the policy off the widget at $k = 4, 5, 6, ...$ and check the first
$k$ where the arrows match $k + 1$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
On this grid, the policy stabilises by $k \approx 5$ — the arrows all point
toward the shortest path to the goal. But the values keep tightening for
many more iterations (≥ 30 to settle to 3 decimals). **Policy convergence is
much faster than value convergence**, because the policy depends only on the
*ranking* of actions, not their absolute values.
</details>

### Problem 4 — How fast does it converge?

We proved $\|v_k - v^*\|_\infty \le \gamma^k \|v_0 - v^*\|_\infty$. With
$\gamma = 0.9$ and starting $v_0 = 0$, how many iterations to reach error
$\le 10^{-3}$? Compare with the widget.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
You need $0.9^k \cdot \|v^* - 0\|_\infty \le 10^{-3}$. Estimate
$\|v^*\|_\infty \le 1/(1 - \gamma) = 10$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$0.9^k \cdot 10 \le 10^{-3} \Rightarrow 0.9^k \le 10^{-4}
\Rightarrow k \ge \log(10^{-4})/\log(0.9) \approx 87$. So in the worst case
roughly 90 iterations. The widget bound is loose because $v^*$ on this grid
is much smaller than 10 (it's about 0.7-1.0), so a few dozen iterations
suffice. **The Banach bound is tight in the worst case but often pessimistic.**
</details>

### Problem 5 — The whole algorithm in one paragraph

Without peeking at *Name*, write the value-iteration algorithm in
pseudocode in 5 lines or fewer.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
```
initialize v ← 0 (or any vector)
repeat:
   for each state s:
      v_new(s) ← max over a of [r(s, a) + γ Σ_{s'} P(s'|s,a) v(s')]
   if ‖v_new − v‖_∞ < ε then stop
   v ← v_new
return: v, and the greedy policy π(s) ← argmax_a [r(s, a) + γ Σ P(s'|s,a) v(s')]
```
That's it. Three lines of math, one stopping criterion, one greedy
extraction. **The simplicity is the point.**
</details>

::: {.key-insight}
Value iteration is "apply $T^*$ until you stop moving." All the magic is in
the contraction property — the algorithm's design is trivial *because* the
proof in Lesson 12 was non-trivial.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

**Value Iteration (VI).** Choose any $v_0 \in \mathbb{R}^{|S|}$, tolerance
$\varepsilon > 0$. Repeat:

$$v_{k+1}(s) \leftarrow \max_a\!\Big[r(s, a) + \gamma\!\sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s, a)\,v_k(s')\Big] \quad \forall s.$$

Stop when $\|v_{k+1} - v_k\|_\infty < \varepsilon$. Return the final $v$ and
the **greedy policy**
$\pi(s) = \arg\max_a [r(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s, a)\,v(s')]$.

By Lesson 12, $v_k \to v^*$ at geometric rate $\gamma^k$ from any $v_0$, and
the returned policy converges to an [optimal policy](/glossary.qmd#optimal-policy).

:::

A verse:

> *Start anywhere. Apply the max-step.*
> *Each pass shrinks error by* $\gamma$.
> *Stop when nothing changes;*
> *Read off the policy by looking greedy.*

## Apply

### Worked example — VI on the 3×3 grid by hand, 3 iterations

Setup: 3×3 grid, goal at $(2,2)$ with reward $+1$ (absorbing), other
rewards $0$, $\gamma = 0.9$. Cells indexed $(\text{row}, \text{col})$ with
$(0,0)$ top-left.

**Initial.** $v_0(s) = 0$ for all $s$.

**Iteration 1.** $T^* v_0(s) = \max_a r(s', a)$. Reward $1$ only at goal.
- $v_1(2, 2) = 1$ (absorbing).
- $v_1(1, 2) = 1$ (one move down → goal, reward 1).
- $v_1(2, 1) = 1$ (one move right → goal, reward 1).
- All other cells: $v_1 = 0$.

**Iteration 2.** $T^* v_1(s) = \max_a [r + \gamma v_1(s')]$.
- $v_2(2, 2) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 1 = 1.9$ (absorbing self-loop).
- $v_2(1, 2) = \max(0 + 0.9 \cdot 1, 1) = 1$ — wait, "down" gives $r = 1$
  (next state is goal) so $1 + 0.9 \cdot 0 = 1$ if we use *yesterday's* $v$
  for the self-loop... actually with absorbing goal, "down from $(1,2)$"
  goes to goal: $r = 1$, $v_1(2,2) = 1$, so $q = 1 + 0.9 = 1.9$. So
  $v_2(1, 2) = 1.9$.
- Similarly $v_2(2, 1) = 1.9$.
- $v_2(0, 2) = \max(0 + 0.9 v_1(\cdot)) = 0.9 \cdot v_1(1, 2) = 0.9 \cdot 1 = 0.9$
  (move down).
- $v_2(1, 1) = 0.9$ (move down or right reaches a cell with $v_1 = 1$).
- $v_2(2, 0) = 0.9$ (move right).
- Others: 0.

**Iteration 3.**
- Goal: $v_3(2,2) = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 1.9 = 2.71$.
- $(1,2), (2,1)$: $v_3 = 1 + 0.9 \cdot 1.9 = 2.71$.
- $(0, 2), (1, 1), (2, 0)$: $v_3 = 0.9 \cdot 1.9 = 1.71$.
- $(0, 1), (1, 0)$: now non-zero — they can reach a $v_2 = 0.9$ cell.
  $v_3(0, 1) = 0.9 \cdot 0.9 = 0.81$, similarly $(1, 0)$.
- $(0, 0)$: still $0$ (its neighbours had $v_2 = 0$).

**Greedy policy after iteration 3.** Already coherent: every cell points
toward the goal along a shortest path. Three iterations suffice for the
*policy*; convergence of *values* takes longer.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** On the same grid, suppose the goal reward is increased to $+10$
(still absorbing). Without redoing the iteration, predict $v_1(1, 2)$ and
$v_2(0, 2)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
The structure is unchanged; only the reward magnitude scales.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v_1(1, 2) = 10$ (one move to goal, reward 10). $v_2(0, 2) = 0 + 0.9 \cdot 10 = 9$.
**Linear in the reward magnitude.** This is why VI is sensitive to reward
*shaping* — same optimal policy, very different value scales.
</details>

**W2.** What does VI return when $\gamma = 0$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$T^* v$ becomes $\max_a r(s, a)$ — the future drops out.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v_1(s) = \max_a r(s, a)$ for every state. Iteration converges in *one step*.
The greedy policy picks the immediate-reward-maximising action — the
"myopic" policy. With $\gamma = 0$, planning collapses to one-step bandit
choice.
</details>

**W3.** Why is the greedy-extraction step at the end a single $\arg\max$,
not another iteration?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Once $v$ is close to $v^*$, the greedy policy w.r.t.\ $v$ is the optimal
policy. Pulling out the policy from $v^*$ takes one $\arg\max$ per state —
no further iteration needed. The hard work was finding $v^*$; reading off
$\pi^*$ is a one-line postprocess.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Run VI on a 1D corridor with 4 cells: states $s_0, s_1, s_2, s_3$,
reward $+1$ at $s_3$ (absorbing), 0 elsewhere, $\gamma = 0.5$, actions
left/right (deterministic). Compute $v_k$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, 3$ and the
final greedy policy.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$v_1(s_2) = 1$ (move right to $s_3$). All others 0.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 2 — the technique</summary>
Each iteration, information propagates one cell to the left.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v_0 = (0, 0, 0, 0)$.
$v_1 = (0, 0, 1, 1)$ — only $s_2, s_3$ touch the goal.
$v_2 = (0, 0.5, 1.5, 1.5)$. ($s_1$ moves right to $s_2$: $0 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$.
$s_2$ self-loop via "right then absorbed": $1 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 1.5$.)
$v_3 = (0.25, 0.75, 1.75, 1.75)$.
At convergence $v^* = (0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0)$ — wait, let me redo: with
$\gamma = 0.5$ and absorbing goal giving reward 1 each step, $v^*(s_3) = 2$.
$v^*(s_2) = 1 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 2$. So actually $v^*(s_2) = v^*(s_3) = 2$,
$v^*(s_1) = 0 + 0.5 \cdot 2 = 1$, $v^*(s_0) = 0.5 \cdot 1 = 0.5$.
**Greedy policy:** all "right." Policy locks after 2 iterations.
</details>

**P2.** Suppose the corridor of P1 has a "trap" at $s_0$: any action from
$s_0$ gives reward $-1$ (and stays at $s_0$). Re-derive $v^*$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$s_0$ is now an absorbing trap. $v^*(s_0)$ satisfies a fixed-point equation
of its own.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$v^*(s_0) = -1 + \gamma v^*(s_0) \Rightarrow v^*(s_0)(1 - 0.5) = -1
\Rightarrow v^*(s_0) = -2$. Other states unchanged because optimal policy
still flees right. **Optimal policies are robust to far-away traps when
$\gamma$ is small.**
</details>

**P3.** *Asynchronous VI.* Instead of computing $v_{k+1}$ from $v_k$ for
*every* state simultaneously, sweep the states one by one and **overwrite
$v$ in place**. Run this on the 4-state corridor of P1. Does it converge
faster?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Sweep right-to-left: update $s_3$, then $s_2$ uses the just-updated $s_3$,
then $s_1$ uses the just-updated $s_2$, etc.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Right-to-left sweep from $v_0 = 0$: $s_3 \to 1$, then $s_2$ uses fresh
$v(s_3) = 1$: $s_2 \to 1 + 0.5 \cdot 1 = 1.5$. Then $s_1 \to 0.5 \cdot 1.5 = 0.75$,
$s_0 \to 0.5 \cdot 0.75 = 0.375$. **One sweep got further than three
synchronous iterations.** This is "Gauss-Seidel VI" (vs.\ "Jacobi VI"); it
converges faster in practice and the contraction proof still applies.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Show that the greedy policy w.r.t.\ $v_k$ is optimal as soon as
$\|v_k - v^*\|_\infty < \Delta_{\min} / (2\gamma)$, where $\Delta_{\min}$
is the smallest gap between optimal and second-best $q^*(s, a)$ over states.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
If $v_k \approx v^*$ within $\epsilon$, then $q$-values w.r.t.\ $v_k$ are
within $\gamma\epsilon$ of $q^*$. If this is less than half the action gap,
the argmax is preserved.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Define $q_k(s, a) = r(s, a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s, a) v_k(s')$.
$|q_k(s, a) - q^*(s, a)| \le \gamma \|v_k - v^*\|_\infty < \Delta_{\min}/2$.
So the optimal action $a^*$ has $q_k(s, a^*) > q^*(s, a^*) - \Delta_{\min}/2$,
while any sub-optimal $a'$ has $q_k(s, a') < q^*(s, a') + \Delta_{\min}/2 \le q^*(s, a^*) - \Delta_{\min}/2$.
So $\arg\max q_k = a^* = \arg\max q^*$. **Policies converge in finitely
many iterations**, even though values converge only asymptotically.
</details>

**S2.** *Vectorise VI.* Write $T^*$ as a vector equation using the matrices
$P_a \in \mathbb{R}^{|S| \times |S|}$ and reward vectors $\mathbf r_a \in \mathbb{R}^{|S|}$
for each action. Implement one VI step in 1-2 lines of NumPy/Julia
pseudocode.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Stack the per-action $q$-vectors into a matrix $Q \in \mathbb{R}^{|S| \times |A|}$,
then take row-wise max.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
For each action $a$: $\mathbf q_a = \mathbf r_a + \gamma P_a \mathbf v$.
Stack: $Q = [\mathbf q_{a_1}, \ldots, \mathbf q_{a_{|A|}}]$.
$\mathbf v_{\text{new}}[s] = \max_a Q[s, a]$. NumPy:
```python
Q = R + gamma * np.einsum('asn,n->as', P, v)   # |A| × |S|
v_new = Q.max(axis=0)
```
This is the kernel of every model-based RL implementation.
</details>

::: {.fun-fact}
Value iteration is *the* algorithm of Bellman's original 1957 paper
*Dynamic Programming*. He had no computers; he ran small examples — three
or four states — by hand. The contraction proof came a decade later
(Denardo 1967), giving a posteriori justification to what Bellman had
shown empirically.
:::

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"VI converges to $v^*$ in finite steps."** No — convergence is
   *asymptotic*, $\gamma^k \to 0$ but never reaches 0. The *policy* converges
   in finite steps (S1), the *values* do not.
2. **"You need to know $v^*$ to compute $v^*$."** No — start anywhere
   ($v_0 = 0$ works) and the contraction pulls you to it.
3. **"VI requires a full sweep."** Async VI (P3) does one state at a time.
   Same convergence guarantee, often faster wall clock.
4. **"$\gamma$ is the same thing as a learning rate."** No — $\gamma$ is a
   property of the *MDP* (how much you care about the future). Learning
   rates show up in stochastic methods (TD, Q-learning), not in VI.
:::

### Takeaways

1. **VI = repeatedly apply $T^*$.** Three lines of code; convergence
   guaranteed by Lesson 12.
2. **Geometric rate $\gamma^k$.** To halve error needs about
   $\log(2)/\log(1/\gamma)$ iterations.
3. **Policy converges before values.** Once $v_k$ is within
   $\Delta_{\min}/(2\gamma)$ of $v^*$, the greedy policy *is* optimal.
4. **Async sweeps work** and often converge faster in practice.

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you write the VI pseudocode without notes?
2. Did you do the worked example (3 iterations on the 3×3 grid) by hand
   before reading it?
3. Can you explain *why* the policy stabilises before the values?

- **3 yes:** move on to Lesson 14 (policy iteration).
- **2 yes:** redo P3 (async VI) — it's the bridge to real implementations.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* and *Apply* with paper. The whole point
  is doing it by hand.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **Lesson 12 (BOE contraction).** Supplies the convergence guarantee.
  Without it, VI is just an algorithm that *might* work; with it, it's
  proven.
- Lesson 9 ([optimal policy](/glossary.qmd#optimal-policy)) supplies the
  notion of greedy extraction.

### What this unlocks

- **Lesson 14 — Policy iteration.** PI alternates *exact* policy evaluation
  with greedy improvement; VI is the special case where evaluation is "one
  Bellman step."
- **Lesson 15 — Truncated PI.** PI and VI are endpoints of a single
  parameterised family.
- **Q-learning (Lesson 26).** The model-free, sampled version of VI.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / ML.** Value iteration is the foundation of every model-based planner
in production: AlphaZero (the search component), MuZero, model-based RL for
robotics. When the model $P$ is known or learned, VI (or one of its
parallel/async variants) is what runs inside.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Systems.** VI shows up in resource allocation (MDP-based scheduling),
inventory control, and queueing — anywhere a finite-state controller must
optimise long-run discounted cost. Bellman's original applications were
mostly in this space.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Sister synthesis lesson.** [`t7.rl-theory`](../../tier-7-masters-tracks/06-rl-theory/index.qmd)
gives the single-paragraph view of reinforcement learning. VI is the
"planning" half of paragraph one.
:::

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- Source: Shiyu Zhao, *Mathematical Foundations of Reinforcement Learning*
  (Springer 2025), §4.1. Cite freely; never transcribe.
- Touchstones: Bellman (the original 1957 algorithm), Sutton & Barto
  (synchronous-vs-async framing), Russian school (the Discover sequence
  forces the algorithm before the box names it).
:::
